He warned against a future where AI giants capture outsized shares of the economy, framing broad value creation and public trust as preconditions for the technology's long-term legitimacy.

title: Google forms research partnership with A24 Films that’s focused on AI filmmaking tools
blurb: Google is investing about $75 million in independent studio A24, giving the film company access to its DeepMind research lab to develop AI tools for the production process. A24 partner Scott Belsky said the work will focus on enhancing workflows like storyboarding rather than generating movies from scratch, marking Google's first investment in a film studio.

title: ‘The claims in the suit are false’: Workday hits back amid lawsuit claiming AI recruitment discrimination
blurb: A US District judge ruled that a class action accusing Workday's AI candidate-screening software of discriminating by age, race, and disability can proceed, making it the first case to test the legality of automated hiring tools. Workday called the claims false and said its tools do not make hiring decisions, while the judge declined to drop an allegation that the system filters candidates using proxy indicators like employment gaps.

title: Menlo Ventures' Ganesan on Anthropic Bet, Backing AI Startups
blurb: Menlo Ventures raised $3B for new AI investments, the largest fundraise in the firm's 50-year history.

title: How to Rank Local LLMs by Cost per Correct Answer (Measured GPU Energy, 8 Ollama Models)
blurb: This post benchmarks 8 local Ollama models on an RTX 3090 by cost per 1,000 correct answers, dividing metered GPU energy by correct responses rather than counting tokens per euro. gemma4:26b won at 96.9% accuracy for €0.013 per 1k-correct, roughly 18x cheaper than qwen3:8b at fp16, which scored 66.7% and showed that reasoning tokens and full precision added cost without improving correctness on these deterministic tasks.

title: Agent Auth: A lawyer’s day in court
blurb: This post frames AI agent authentication through the analogy of a lawyer representing a client in court. It maps four requirements onto courtroom steps: agent identity (proving who the agent is), principal identity (which user it acts for), delegation via On-Behalf-Of (OBO) tokens carrying scoped permissions, and policy enforcement that validates each action against applicable rules. The argument is that agents are microservices-plus, demanding stronger auth, policy, and observability than traditional services.

title: Robinhood launches agentic trading via MCP server for all customers
blurb: Robinhood launched agentic trading through a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server, making it available to all customers. The integration lets AI agents connect directly to Robinhood accounts to execute trades and access portfolio data, extending agent-driven workflows into retail brokerage for the first time at this scale.

title: Only half of US datacenter capacity planned for 2026 is actually  under construction
blurb: A report found that only about half of the US datacenter capacity slated to come online in 2026 is currently under construction, suggesting a wide gap between announced buildout plans and physical reality. The shortfall raises questions about whether projected AI compute supply will materialize on schedule, even as demand forecasts and capital commitments continue to climb.

title: Why the Human Genome's Tangled Physicality May Confound AI
blurb: The author argues that the human genome resists the kind of understanding biologists once expected, since barely 2% of its 3 billion bases code for genes and the real difficulty lies in how those genes are regulated. Genomic foundation models like Evo 2, Genos, and DeepMind's AlphaGenome sidestep that complexity by learning correlations from known cases rather than mechanisms. The piece contends this will likely prove useful for prediction while leaving the deeper question of how the genome works inside a computational black box.

title: Anthropic's Mythos mess just keeps getting more complicated
blurb: The Trump administration issued an export control barring Anthropic's Mythos 5 and its Fable 5 derivative from use by any foreign national, including Anthropic's own employees, prompting the company to pull both models for all customers. The Register's reporting examines Amazon's possible role in justifying the move and notes that open-weight models will likely replicate the same capabilities regardless of the restriction.

title: Sorry, Slackbot. Claude is taking your job
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Tag (@Claude) in research preview, an always-on Slack assistant with a single shared identity that anyone in a channel can summon by typing @Claude. Admins control its access to tools and data per channel, and an optional "ambient" mode lets it proactively surface relevant information and follow up on stalled tasks. It is launching first for Claude Enterprise and Team customers.

title: When I reject AI code even if it works
blurb: The author argues that as AI accelerates implementation, the real bottleneck shifts to reviewing code the developer never reasoned through, producing cognitive overload that makes him reject working changes and start over. He rejects AI code when he can't explain the approach in his own words, when the diff exceeds the problem, or when it adds unproven abstractions, advocating mandatory human review alongside AI checks.

title: Do AI Assistants Actually Render Your JavaScript when Grounding? We Put It to the Test
blurb: A test of 12 AI assistants found that most read only raw HTML and miss JavaScript-rendered content when grounding on a pasted link.

title: 🎙️ How I AI: How to write AI agent loops in Claude Code and Codex + How Claude Mythos found a 15-year-old bug in Mozilla Firefox
blurb: A How I AI tutorial breaking down heartbeats, crons, hooks, and goal-based agent loops, with real automations built in Claude Code and Codex.

title: This sneaky photo trick gets AI chatbots to ignore their safety rules
blurb: Researchers at Florida International University developed JaiLIP, a jailbreak method that hides instructions in images through pixel-level changes invisible to humans but readable by multimodal AI models. Testing on BLIP-2, altered images nearly doubled the rate of harmful responses, and the team found small language models used for business tasks like bookkeeping and customer support especially easy to fool.

title: NVIDIA announces 35 new AI HPC supercomputers across Europe
blurb: NVIDIA announced 35 new AI HPC supercomputers opening across Europe this year, built on its Blackwell and Hopper architectures and serving more than three million researchers. The systems span projects like Barcelona's MareNostrum 5 upgrade and Italy's IT4LIA AI factory, supporting research in climate science, healthcare, clean energy, and quantum computing.

title: SpaceX to buy Cursor AI coding agent operator Anysphere for $60B
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire Anysphere, the company behind the Cursor AI coding agent, for $60B. The deal would fold one of the most widely used AI coding tools into Elon Musk's space and satellite empire, signaling SpaceX's push to build out internal AI software capabilities and developer infrastructure.

title: Report: GKE Inference Gateway delivers up to 92% faster AI responses
blurb: Google's GKE Inference Gateway uses prefix caching and model-aware routing to direct LLM requests to accelerators already holding the relevant KV cache in memory, skipping recomputation of repeated prompt prefixes. An independent benchmark found it delivered 15.7% higher throughput, 92.8% shorter wait times, and 62.6% lower inter-token latency than the next leading managed Kubernetes service, with Snap reporting prefix cache hit rates of 75-80%.

title: Stripe and AWS enable AI agent payments for content owners and publishers
blurb: Stripe and AWS introduced infrastructure that lets AI agents make payments on behalf of users while compensating the content owners and publishers whose material the agents access. The integration combines Stripe's payment rails with AWS Bedrock and AgentCore, giving publishers a way to meter and monetize agent traffic rather than have their content scraped for free.

title: Evals: the strategic IP that will define the next era of AI
blurb: Garrett Lord argues that internal evaluations, not models, are becoming the strategic IP that will determine which companies win the next era of AI. Drawing on conversations with hundreds of execs frustrated by weak AI ROI, he contends that proprietary evals encoding a company's quality standards are what turn general-purpose models into reliable, differentiated production systems.

title: UK funds £60M AI labs to challenge US tech dominance with open-source models
blurb: The UK government awarded £60M to two new academic research labs at Oxford and UCL, focused on open-source, low-hardware AI architectures to reduce reliance on US tech firms. The funding runs over six years, up from an initial plan for a single £40M lab, with £2M per lab reserved for hiring at least ten doctoral students each.

title: Why corporate AI super PACs spent $27 million on a local election
blurb: Corporate AI super PACs spent $27.83M to influence tonight's NY-12 House primary in Manhattan, with money flowing on both sides of the race. OpenAI-aligned groups backed one candidate while crypto billionaire Chris Larsen and Anthropic-linked PACs, including one funded by a $20M Anthropic donation, lined up behind progressive assemblyman Alex Bores.

title: As Chinese Tech Pulls Ahead, U.S. Fears It Will Become Dependent
blurb: U.S. officials warn that growing reliance on advanced Chinese technology, from manufacturing equipment to industrial systems, could leave American industry dependent on a strategic rival. The concern marks a shift from earlier fears about China copying U.S. innovation to one in which Chinese firms increasingly lead in key sectors and Washington must decide whether to use their products.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Tag enterprise collaborative tool for agentic workflows
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Tag, a beta tool for Enterprise and Team customers that lets people delegate tasks to Claude by tagging it as a team member in Slack channels. Described as an evolution of Claude Code, the tool builds context from the channels it joins, can schedule and automate tasks, and replaces the existing Claude in Slack integration on August 3.

title: Google DeepMind bets $75M on AI’s future in Hollywood with A24 deal
blurb: Google DeepMind invested $75M in indie studio A24 to co-develop AI filmmaking tools, with Demis Hassabis framing it as a first-of-its-kind partnership that gives DeepMind direct feedback from working artists. A24, the studio behind "Everything Everywhere All At Once" and "Backrooms," joins a pattern already set by Netflix buying Affleck's InterPositive and Amazon's MGM standing up an AI production unit. The frontier labs are no longer just selling models to Hollywood, they are buying their way into the creative process to source the proprietary taste and feedback data that generic models lack.

title: The PIK Fuse: How Private Equity Software Deals Like Medallia Actually Blow Up, and Exactly Who’s Next
blurb: Private equity loaded a dozen software leaders with debt at the 2021 peak, and the PIK toggle is what hides the damage until it's terminal. Payment-in-kind lets a borrower defer cash interest and roll it onto the principal, so the balance compounds while the business weakens and covenant tests still pass right up until the deferral window closes. Thoma Bravo just handed Medallia to its lenders, wiping out $5.1B in equity on a company earning $200M a year against $300M in debt service. It is the second giant wipeout in 18 months, and the fuse runs the same way every time.

title: 21,000 Oracle jobs vanish amid Big Red's big bets on AI
blurb: Oracle's headcount fell from 162,000 to 141,000 over the past year, a cut of about 21,000 jobs, even as the company pours billions into datacenter expansion. The spending and the layoffs point the same direction: Oracle is reallocating payroll into AI compute capacity, betting that GPUs and datacenters generate more return than the workers it shed. For founders, it's the clearest signal yet that incumbents will trade headcount for capex when they think AI infrastructure is the higher-leverage bet.

title: Trump says he no longer views Anthropic as a national security threat after G7 meeting with CEO
blurb: Trump told Axios he no longer sees Anthropic as a national security threat and may ease the June 12 Commerce directive forcing the company to seek US approval before foreign nationals can touch its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models. The reversal came days after a G7 meeting with Dario Amodei, capping a three-month standoff that started when the Pentagon flagged Anthropic as a supply-chain risk over its refusal to strip military guardrails. The thaw lands right as Anthropic sits on a confidential IPO filing, where regulatory overhang is exactly the kind of risk that scares public-market buyers.

title: AI chipmaker Groq confirms $650M raise, re-staffs after Nvidia’s $20B not-acqui-hire deal
blurb: Six months after Nvidia licensed Groq's chip IP and hired away its CEO and top executives in a $20B not-acqui-hire, Groq confirmed a $650M round led by Disruptive and Infinitum. Rather than wind down, the company pivoted to its neocloud business, which now runs 13 data centers and serves over five million developers, and restocked its bench with a new COO, CTO, and CPO. The not-acqui-hire is becoming a recurring exit shape, where a rival buys the IP and the talent while investors cash out, and the open question is whether the husk left behind can rebuild into a real business.

title: MGX’s $50 Billion Raise Sets Up One of the Biggest AI Funds Ever
blurb: Abu Dhabi's MGX raised close to $50B from sovereign wealth funds, global pension funds, and large institutional investors to spend on AI infrastructure and technology. That puts one of the largest pools of dedicated AI capital ever assembled in the hands of a single state-backed buyer, on top of MGX's existing role in Stargate and OpenAI's funding. For founders building infrastructure-heavy AI, the marginal dollar increasingly comes from sovereign balance sheets, which changes who you raise from and the terms that come with it.

title: Stark, which has a deal to supply "kamikaze" drones to the German military, raised €500M from Founders Fund, Sequoia, others; it faced criticism over Thiel ties (Financial Times)
blurb: Stark raised €500M from Founders Fund, Sequoia, and others to supply "kamikaze" drones to the German military, even as it drew criticism over its Peter Thiel ties. The round lands as European defense tech pulls in record venture money, with startups racing to rearm the continent amid sustained pressure on NATO budgets. The backlash signals the cost founders now weigh when building in defense: the capital is flowing, but the politics around who funds the weapons travel with the deal.

title: Inference chip startup Groq raises $650M to grow its cloud platform
blurb: Groq raised $650M to expand its inference cloud, seven months after licensing its LPU chip technology to Nvidia in a $20B deal that also took its founding CEO. The bet is that the value sits in operating the inference, not owning the silicon: Groq's platform now runs trillions of tokens a week for 5 million developers across 13 data centers. The proceeds go toward scaling capacity to 200 megawatts by 2027, even as Nvidia ships the same LPU 3 hardware and rival clouds can theoretically stand up competing services.

title: Nvidia backs AI networking switch silicon startup Upscale in $190M raise
blurb: Upscale AI raised $190M to build networking switch silicon aimed only at AI workloads, bringing total funding to $500M and its valuation to $2B barely a year after launching. The round was led by Premji Invest, but the notable name is Nvidia, which joined the extension even as it sells its own AI networking gear. A frontier supplier funding a startup that competes in part of its own stack signals how acute the cluster-scale networking bottleneck has become, and how much room there still is below the incumbents.

title: Oracle cut 21,000 jobs this year and its SEC filing explicitly blames AI
blurb: Oracle's headcount fell to 141,000 from 162,000 over the past year, a net cut of roughly 21,000, and its annual SEC filing states plainly that AI adoption "have resulted, and may continue to result, in reductions to our workforce." It is a rare case of a major tech company putting the AI-replaces-jobs argument into a securities disclosure rather than an earnings call, where lawyers are comfortable telling regulators what most CEOs only imply. The deepest cuts hit Oracle Health and legacy SaaS teams, while Cloud Infrastructure and AI roles were spared or expanded.

title: How Anthropic may have talked itself into an AI export ban
blurb: Anthropic spent 2026 warning about AI risk far more than any rival, and the bet may be backfiring. FT analysis found 5 of every 1,000 words from the company and Dario Amodei related to risk, regulation, or restrictions, eight times OpenAI's 0.6. Critics now blame that messaging for Washington's decision last week to bar foreign nationals from using Anthropic's newest models, Mythos and Fable. The fight is an early test of how the US will govern frontier models, and a warning that loud safety positioning can invite the regulation it asks for.

title: Sources: Abu Dhabi's MGX has raised close to $50B from regional sovereign wealth funds, global pension funds, and others to accelerate spending on AI deals (Dinesh Nair/Bloomberg)
blurb: Abu Dhabi's MGX has raised close to $50B from regional sovereign wealth funds, global pension funds, and other backers to accelerate its AI dealmaking. The fund is assembling one of the largest pools of patient capital aimed squarely at compute, infrastructure, and frontier-lab stakes, where check sizes already run into the billions. State-backed money on this scale resets who sets terms in the biggest AI rounds, and founders raising at the frontier will increasingly find a sovereign fund, not a traditional VC, anchoring the deal.

title: SpaceX Strikes $6 Billion Deal With AI Startup for Data-Center Space
blurb: SpaceX signed a $6B deal to rent data-center capacity to Reflection AI, which will use it to train open-source models. The move puts a rocket company in the compute-landlord business and gives a young lab access to scale without building its own infrastructure. It is another sign that the AI buildout is being financed through long-term capacity contracts, where whoever controls power and data-center space increasingly sets the terms for the labs renting it.

title: Walmart acquires Vibe.co, which lets businesses create and buy ads on CTVs, sources say for $1.4B cash; top executives get $180M to stay for four years (Sarah Nassauer/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Walmart acquired Vibe.co, which lets businesses create and buy ads on connected TVs, in a deal sources put at $1.4B cash, with top executives getting $180M to stay four years. The retailer is buying its way deeper into ad tech, the highest-margin layer of its business, where it already commands shopper data that pure-play CTV platforms can't match. For founders building on the retail-media stack, your distribution partner is now assembling the full ad pipeline in-house.

title: Sequoia, Founders Fund Back Kamikaze Drone Startup Stark at €3.5 Billion Valuation
blurb: Stark Defence, a two-year-old Berlin maker of kamikaze drones, raised €500M ($570M) at a €3.2B valuation in a round led by Sequoia and Founders Fund. The company had raised just €140M total before this, so the new round marks a roughly 20x step up in committed capital as US venture firms move aggressively into European defense. Sequoia and Thiel backing a German drone startup, alongside the NATO Innovation Fund, signals that military hardware has become a core venture category rather than a fringe bet.

title: The minimum viable unit of saleable software
blurb: LLMs flipped the buy-vs-build calculus, so the cheap internal package that replaces your $400/mo SaaS is now a real threat to anyone selling software. But cheap isn't zero, and the author's point is that LLM-built systems still demand dozens of feedback loops to get right plus an open-ended maintenance bill for every new feature and bug. The opening for saleable software is the work LLMs make easier but not free, where the ongoing cost of owning the thing yourself still outweighs the price of buying it.

title: Micron invests in Anthropic and grants it a supply deal
blurb: Micron invested in Anthropic's Series H round and signed a multi-year deal to supply it with HBM, DRAM, and SSDs, with Samsung and SK Hynix also joining the round. The flow runs backwards from the usual buyer-supplier relationship: the memory vendor is putting capital into its own customer to lock in the relationship as Anthropic scales compute toward an IPO. Securing memory allocation, not just GPUs, is now a frontier-lab bottleneck, and suppliers are paying for a seat at the table rather than waiting to be paid.

title: Menlo raised $3B for funds dedicated to backing AI startups, its largest fundraising to date; sources say Menlo's Anthropic stake is currently worth nearly $14B (Natasha Mascarenhas/Bloomberg)
blurb: Menlo Ventures raised $3B for funds dedicated to backing AI startups, its largest haul ever, while sources peg its Anthropic stake at nearly $14B. A single early bet now dwarfs the new fund, which is the pattern reshaping how venture firms raise and deploy. The math pushes more capital toward a handful of frontier labs and the startups orbiting them, leaving founders outside that gravity well competing for attention from investors who already have their generational winner.

title: Groq Raises $650 Million to Aid in Pivot After Nvidia Deal
blurb: Groq raised $650M to expand its data center capacity and reposition from a chip startup into a full AI compute provider, following its recent deal with Nvidia. The pivot reflects where the margins are moving: selling inference capacity as a service rather than competing head-on in silicon against the company that just became a partner. For founders building on hosted inference, it signals more competition among compute suppliers and likely downward pressure on per-token pricing.

title: AI's Affordability Crisis
blurb: The economics under AI usage are upside down: a $200-a-month subscription lets a heavy user burn roughly $8,000 in Anthropic tokens or $14,000 in OpenAI tokens, meaning the labs subsidize some enterprise customers by 40 to 70 times. Estimates put the platforms at spending $8 to $14 to generate every $1 of revenue, and one analysis found gross margins go negative once a user touches just 25% of their rate limit. The demand is real because the price is fake, and that gap is being filled by equity raises rather than working economics.

title: Drones startup Stark confirms €500m raise backed by Sequoia and Founders Fund
blurb: Berlin-based Stark confirmed a €500m raise to scale European defence manufacturing, backed by Sequoia, Founders Fund, the NATO Innovation Fund, and Advent. More than 80% of the capital goes straight into R&D and production, including new electronic warfare facilities and a target of thousands of drone systems per month. Founded in 2024 and already past unicorn status, Stark has signed a €268m German contract and expanded into the UK and Sweden. The signal for founders is that European defence has become a venture category that scales like software, with frontier funds writing nine-figure checks.

title: Sources: Meta is building a standalone prediction markets app called Arena, which would probably rely on video game-like points instead of money wagers (New York Times)
blurb: Meta is building a standalone prediction markets app called Arena, according to the New York Times, and it would likely run on video game-style points rather than real money wagers. The points-not-cash design keeps Meta clear of the gambling regulations that govern rivals like Kalshi and Polymarket, while still capturing the engagement that prediction markets generate. It also signals that Meta sees forecasting as a native social mechanic, another surface to pull attention back into its apps as it competes for the same audience that has been migrating toward standalone betting and prediction platforms.

title: SpaceX signs computing power deal with open-source AI startup Reflection worth up to $6.3 billion
blurb: SpaceX signed a computing deal with open-source startup Reflection worth up to $6.3 billion, giving the $25 billion lab immediate access to Nvidia GB300s for $150 million a month through 2029. The deal turns Colossus, built to train Musk's Grok, into a compute landlord that already counts Anthropic, Google, and Cursor as customers. The timing matters: open-source momentum has spiked since Anthropic cut off Fable and Mythos, and Reflection is betting that governments and enterprises wary of closed-model lock-in will pay for models they can inspect, customize, and run themselves.

title: Sources: SpaceX, which is seeking to raise between $20B and $25B in its debut US bond sale, has drawn about $89B of demand (Bloomberg)
blurb: SpaceX drew about $89B of demand for its debut US bond sale, more than three times the $20B to $25B it set out to raise. The oversubscription signals that fixed-income investors now treat a private, pre-IPO company as blue-chip credit, a status usually reserved for public mega-caps. For founders, it marks how far the largest private companies can now reach into debt markets, raising at scale without ceding equity or facing the disclosure that a public listing would force.

title: Early Anthropic Backer Menlo Lands $3 Billion in Its Largest-Ever Haul
blurb: Menlo Ventures raised $3 billion across a set of AI-focused funds, its largest haul ever, on the back of a single bet-the-firm wager. Its $1 billion in Anthropic stakes, started in 2024 when the lab had little revenue, is now worth nearly $14 billion as Anthropic's valuation cleared $900 billion. That outcome is reshaping the firm's strategy, pushing a historically early-stage investor toward more Anthropic-sized later-stage checks.

title: Meta announces new smart glasses starting at $299, as Zuckerberg keeps pushing wearables
blurb: Meta released a $299 smart glasses model, at least $80 below its second-gen Ray-Ban entry price, dropping the Ray-Ban and Oakley branding while keeping the EssilorLuxottica partnership. Meta and EssilorLuxottica already own more than 80% of a still-small market, and the cheaper, screenless camera-and-speaker device is a deliberate step toward lens-display computing like the $799 Ray-Ban Display. The contest is now forming around price tiers, as Google builds Gemini eyewear with Warby Parker and Snap pitches $2,195 Specs as the smartphone's successor.

title: Building a ‘digital twin’ 10,000 feet underground: PNNL, Nvidia and Fervo team up on geothermal AI
blurb: Geothermal just got a serious compute backer: PNNL, Nvidia, and Fervo are building a publicly available digital twin that models reservoirs 10,000 feet down to optimize how operators drill, monitor, and inject water. Today's models are too slow to guide real-time decisions, which leaves heat in the ground and capacity stranded. The bet matters because data centers are starving for clean baseload power, and Fervo, fresh off $2.17B raised, is bringing a 500-megawatt Utah plant online this year while Nvidia supplies the infrastructure and Fervo the proprietary field data.

title: AI memory startup focused on cutting token costs raises $98 million
blurb: Engram, an 8-month-old startup that builds a "learned memory" layer for AI, raised $98 million from General Catalyst, Kleiner Perkins, Sequoia, and Andrej Karpathy. It recalls organization-specific workflows and context to match or beat frontier labs using up to 100 times fewer tokens, and already counts Microsoft, Notion, and Harvey as clients. The bet runs against the old assumption that scale lowers cost, because newer models keep getting pricier, and the money is moving to the layer that makes existing models cheaper rather than the labs racing to build bigger ones.

title: 7 Mistakes Founders Make When Chasing Their First Deals
blurb: Most early founders stall on the same two mistakes: waiting for a perfect product before selling, and pricing themselves into irrelevance. Selling is a contact sport, so the faster move is to ship a hypothesis about a real problem and let customer conversations correct your aim, because early signals are what get you to product-market fit. On price, charging one or two thousand a month is pocket change to a serious buyer. If the number is that small, run it as a design partnership and charge real money once you have proof.

title: Tesla plans to sell modular AI data center hardware called ‘Megapod'
blurb: Tesla filed an intent-to-use trademark for "Megapod," a self-contained modular AI data center system bundling servers, networking, power distribution, and cooling into a single turnkey unit. The move comes less than a year after Tesla killed Dojo, its only in-house AI training computer, and pushes it into a market Nvidia already dominates with the GB200 NVL72 and DGX SuperPOD, the reference designs Dell and Supermicro both build on. Tesla would be selling the rack-and-room around chips it doesn't make, against the company whose silicon powers essentially all of it.

title: Meta Has Created a Prediction Markets App
blurb: Meta built an experimental prediction markets app, internally called Arena, that would run independently of Facebook and Instagram. It puts Meta in direct competition with Polymarket and Kalshi, the two largest players in a category that has drawn regulatory scrutiny and surging volume over the past year. The move signals Meta is again chasing standalone consumer products outside its core social apps, betting that prediction markets become the next mainstream venue for attention and engagement.

title: Oracle shed about 20k roles globally in the last year
blurb: Oracle cut about 21,000 roles in the past year, roughly 13% of its workforce, ending May 2026 with 141,000 full-time staff after explicitly tying the reductions to AI deployment across its operations. The move cost $1.8B in severance and restructuring, nearly five times the prior year's bill, as the company pours at least $50B into data centers for customers like OpenAI and Meta. More than 100,000 tech workers have been laid off in the past year, and the pattern is consistent: the same firms funding the AI buildout are shrinking headcount to pay for it.

title: SpaceX lands $6.3B compute deal with open-source AI startup Reflection and Nvidia is on both sides of the trade
blurb: SpaceX just became one of the largest compute landlords in AI, signing Reflection AI to a $6.3B deal that pays $150M a month for Nvidia GB300 chips at its Colossus 2 site through 2029. That pushes SpaceX's committed compute revenue from outside clients past $80B, alongside Anthropic, Google, and the Cursor team it is acquiring for $60B. Nvidia sits on both sides of the trade, selling the GPUs that fill Colossus and holding a stake in the labs renting them, which is how the same chips get paid for twice.

title: GEO is following the same path as early SEO
blurb: GEO, the practice of getting your brand mentioned in AI-generated answers, is running the same playbook early SEO did, and the same shakeout is coming. The durable tactics are the boring ones: high-quality content that answers real prompts, FAQs, TL;DR summaries, question-based headlines, and clean schema. The black hat shortcuts that work right now, like AI spam, fake reviews to game E-E-A-T, and cloaking content for crawlers, are the ones SEO history says get penalized out of existence. Build for the version of GEO that survives the cleanup, not the gold rush that funds it today.

title: AppsFlyer Reportedly Lands $1B At $2.7B Valuation To Help Companies Track Digital Ads
blurb: AppsFlyer reportedly raised more than $1B at a $2.7B valuation, its first round since 2020 and a step the company says is on its path to the public markets. The telling detail is who wrote the checks: Moloco, Google, Meta, and Unity each took a minority stake, meaning the ad platforms being measured are now funding their own independent referee. As AI takes over how ads get bought and optimized, the attribution signals feeding those systems become infrastructure worth owning, which is why the measurement layer is suddenly drawing nine-figure rounds.

title: Alibaba Sues the US, Seeking Removal From Pentagon’s Blacklist
blurb: Alibaba sued the US Department of Defense to get off the Pentagon's list of companies that allegedly support the Chinese military, calling the designation arbitrary and unsupported by evidence. The blacklist doesn't ban sales, but it brands a company as a national-security risk, which scares off US partners and weighs on its standing with American investors. Alibaba is betting the US courts will overturn a national-security call that the executive branch usually wins, a fight that signals how far Chinese tech firms will now go to defend access to Western markets.

title: SpaceX Draws $89 Billion Demand for Debut Bond Offering
blurb: SpaceX drew about $89B of demand for its debut US bond sale, oversubscribing a $20B to $25B target by more than four times. The notable part is who showed up: bond investors are structurally more conservative than equity buyers, yet they are betting on Musk even as SpaceX is projected to burn significant cash over the next few years. When the most risk-averse pool of capital lines up four-deep for an unprofitable company, it signals that frontier hardware and AI infrastructure are now investment-grade in the market's eyes, not just venture bets.

title: Stark Defence raises €500M led by Sequoia and Founders Fund at a valuation above €3.5 billion
blurb: Stark Defence raised €500M led by Sequoia and Founders Fund at a valuation above €3.5B, roughly triple where it sat earlier this year and just two years after founding. The Berlin maker of loitering munitions has now pulled in more than €660M total, with over 80% of the new capital going to manufacturing, after Germany awarded it and rival Helsing initial drone contracts worth about €269M each. European defence tech is now drawing software-scale rounds, and the capital is flowing to companies that can actually build hardware at volume.

title: Backblaze gets $335 million CoreWeave bonanza
blurb: Backblaze signed a five-year, multi-exabyte deal with neocloud CoreWeave worth $335M to supply HDD-based storage tiers in CoreWeave's AI Object Storage. The deal tracks a quiet economic shift under the AI buildout: flash now runs about 10 times more expensive per terabyte than hard drives, and neoclouds storing data at exabyte scale can't sustain all-flash architectures. The labs and their suppliers are discovering that the unglamorous layer, cheap durable storage, is what keeps expensive compute from sitting idle, and the storage vendors are moving to claim it.

title: Bristol fusion startup raises £23M to produce cancer-fighting isotopes while the rest of the industry is still 20 years away
blurb: Bristol-based Astral Systems raised £23M in a Series A led by Mercia Ventures to scale production of medical isotopes for cancer diagnosis and treatment. While most fusion companies are still two decades from generating power, Astral already runs reactors and has booked more than £3M from research contracts, betting that revenue today beats the long wait for fusion electricity. The market is real and underserved, as more than 50 million nuclear medicine procedures a year depend on a fragile global isotope supply chain with no reliable UK domestic source.

title: SpaceX’s Shortest-Dated Debt Is Biggest Lure in $25 Billion Sale
blurb: SpaceX is raising $25B in its debut bond sale across five tranches, paying a relatively wide premium over Treasuries even after pulling in roughly $89B in demand. The shortest-dated notes drew the most interest, a sign that investors will lend to Musk's rocket-satellite-AI conglomerate but want to stay liquid rather than commit long. Coming weeks after the biggest-ever IPO, the deal shows the equity market's enthusiasm doesn't transfer cleanly to credit, where buyers price the actual cash flows and ask harder questions about a capital-hungry business.

title: Menlo Ventures' Ganesan on Anthropic Bet, Backing AI Startups
blurb: Menlo Ventures raised $3 billion for new AI investments, the largest fundraise in the firm's 50-year history. The size signals how the venture model is reordering itself around a handful of AI bets, with capital concentrating in firms that backed the labs early. Menlo's Anthropic stake is the template here, and the new fund is a wager that the next round of winners will come from the startups building on top of those labs rather than the labs themselves.

title: Supreme Court Puts Curbs on Human-Rights Suits in Cisco Win
blurb: The US Supreme Court narrowed the legal exposure companies face for human-rights abuses abroad, throwing out a suit that accused Cisco of helping China surveil and torture Falun Gong members. The decision continues a years-long line of rulings that has steadily gutted the Alien Tort Statute, the 1789 law human-rights advocates have used to pull multinationals into US courts over conduct overseas. For founders building surveillance, security, or infrastructure tools sold into authoritarian markets, the practical liability for how those products get used just got materially smaller.

title: SpaceX Loses $600 Billion in Value Over Three Days
blurb: SpaceX shed more than $600B in market value over a three-day selloff, though shares still sit roughly 10% above the $135 IPO price. The drop wasn't company-specific news but a cautious turn in the broader AI trade, per New Street Research's Pierre Ferragu. Newly public companies tethered to the AI narrative now move on sentiment about the trade itself, not their own fundamentals, which means founders eyeing a near-term IPO are pricing in a market mood they don't control.

title: Revolut, Wise and UiPath-backer Seedcamp raises $320m
blurb: Seedcamp, the European early-stage firm behind Revolut, Wise, and UiPath, raised $320m across two new funds to keep writing first checks into the region's startups. The firm is leaning into the sovereignty wave sweeping European tech, but drawing a hard line: it won't back companies whose only differentiator is being local. The signal for founders is that capital is flowing again to European pre-seed, but the bar is the same as anywhere, and "we're sovereign" is a feature, not a business.

title: Lama AI raises $10M to accelerate automated loan originations
blurb: Lama AI raised $10M in a Series A led by EJF Ventures, bringing its total to more than $20M after tripling revenue in the past year. The startup builds an AI-native loan origination platform that points autonomous agents at intake, document collection, underwriting, approvals, and portfolio monitoring, aimed at community and regional banks. The bet is small-business lending, where underwriting a small loan costs about as much as a large one, so most banks cap their volume and leave borrower demand unmet. Lama AI argues automation changes that math.

title: Gracias’ Valor to Raise $2.5 Billion Fund Amid SpaceX Win
blurb: Antonio Gracias' Valor Equity Partners is raising about $2.5 billion for its seventh fund, with part already allocated to shares of the newly public SpaceX. Valor's long alliance with Elon Musk is now paying off in liquidity, as SpaceX began trading June 12 and turned a decade of insider positioning into a marketable return. The fund is a reminder that in venture, durable access to a single generational company can matter more than a broad portfolio, and the firms with the closest founder relationships are the ones cashing in.

title: Bristol’s Astral Systems secures €26 million Series A to support medical isotope commercialisation
blurb: Astral Systems raised €26 million in a Series A led by Mercia Ventures to commercialise medical isotopes from its compact multi-state fusion reactors by early 2027. The Bristol DeepTech firm is betting fusion's near-term payoff is not energy but the radioisotope supply chain, where global shortages routinely stall cancer diagnosis and treatment. It plans to run next-generation reactors at full capacity at the former Berkeley Power Station by the end of 2026, aiming to revive domestic UK isotope production rather than chase grid power.

title: Why Europe is rebuilding its tech stack
blurb: European CTOs are now under board-level pressure to rip US technology out of their stacks, and a single event in June made the threat concrete. The Trump administration ordered Anthropic to block foreign nationals from its most powerful models, a live demonstration of the "digital kill switch" that turned a policy debate into an operational risk. The dependency runs deep: a Proton survey found 74% of publicly listed European companies rely on US-made email alone, and Denmark signups doubled after Trump's Greenland threats. Sovereignty has moved from white paper to migration plan.

title: Stark bags €500M in new funding
blurb: Stark, the German maker of weaponized drones, raised €500M at a €3.5B valuation, with Sequoia, Founders Fund, the NATO Innovation Fund, and Peter Thiel among the backers. More than 80% of the capital goes straight into R&D and manufacturing, funding electronic warfare research and production scale-up. Founded in 2024, Stark has become the poster child for a European defense tech surge driven by governments ramping up military spend.

title: Built by a tradesman backed by a16z and Sequoia, Probook raises $40M to reinvent dispatch for America’s home service businesses
blurb: Probook raised $40M to automate the unglamorous core of home services: dispatch. The $34M Series A was led by a16z, on top of a $6M seed led by Sequoia, and the pitch is an AI operating system that handles scheduling, customer communication, and routing for plumbing, HVAC, and electrical operators. While most startups chased lead generation and customer acquisition, Probook went after operational execution, and the early numbers show why, with some customers running technician-to-dispatcher ratios as high as 100:1.

title: US Chip Stocks Plunge as AI Selloff Ripples Across From Asia
blurb: A global selloff in chipmaker shares spread from Asia to the US, with the Nasdaq 100 down 2.5% as investors questioned whether the AI-fueled rally can hold. Nvidia and Micron pulled the S&P 500 down about 1%, though defensive sectors like consumer staples and real estate cushioned the slide and the Dow eked out a 0.2% gain. The split tape is the signal worth watching: capital is rotating out of the AI trade and into safety, the first broad market test of how much of the rally was priced on faith.

title: EU Sees 6 Billion Reasons for New Levy on Online Shoppers
blurb: The EU will impose a €3 duty on imported parcels under €150 starting July 1, a move aimed squarely at the flood of low-cost goods from Shein, Temu, and other China-based sellers. The bloc expects the levy to raise around €6 billion while blunting the cost advantage that built those marketplaces in Europe. For any founder whose unit economics lean on cheap cross-border fulfillment, the era of frictionless sub-€150 shipping into the EU is closing, and pricing models built on that gap will need rework.

title: Europe’s biggest tech CEOs have formed a standing lobby group with a direct line to von der Leyen
blurb: Seven of Europe's largest tech companies, including ASML, Airbus, Ericsson, and Mistral, have formed a standing lobby group called the European Tech Creators with a direct line to Commission President von der Leyen. The group represents €417 billion in annual revenue and nearly €1.1 trillion in market cap, and it is pushing for faster deregulation, easier mergers, and a completed single market. It already appears to be working, as the EU moved toward softening the AI Act within a week of the group's late-April meeting, postponing high-risk obligations by 16 months.

title: Astral Systems raises £23M to tackle the global shortages of medical radioisotopes
blurb: Astral Systems raised £23M, led by Mercia Ventures, to mass-produce medical radioisotopes from compact fusion reactors rather than chase long-term power. The distinction is the bet: while most fusion ventures sell a decade-out promise, Astral already runs multiple TRL9 reactors and plans to ship critical medical isotopes by early 2027. The supply chain for these isotopes is fragile and prone to global shortages that stall cancer diagnosis and treatment, so a continuously operable private neutron source at the old Berkeley Power Station is a near-term commercial product, not a science project.

title: JUPUS raises €13M to power the next generation of AI-driven law firms
blurb: JUPUS raised €13M in a Series A led by Semapa Next to build AI secretarial software for law firms. The pitch tracks a structural gap: over the past three decades, newly trained legal assistants in Germany fell more than 70%, while practising lawyers tripled and admin workloads kept climbing, hitting the small and mid-sized firms that make up most of Europe's legal market. JUPUS automates calls, intake, case prep, and drafting for more than 2,000 lawyers and 2,000 daily cases, and quadrupled ARR in 2025 as it sells the staffing shortfall as the wedge.

title: Run isolated sandboxes with full lifecycle control: AWS Lambda introduces MicroVMs
blurb: AWS Lambda introduced MicroVMs, a serverless primitive that runs untrusted user- or AI-generated code in isolated, stateful environments powered by Firecracker, the same virtualization behind Lambda functions. Each MicroVM gives a session VM-level isolation with near-instant launch and resume, retains memory and disk state for the session, and pauses to low idle cost when the user steps away.

title: Researchers Detail DifyTap Flaws in Dify That Could Expose AI Chats Across Tenants
blurb: Zafran Security disclosed four vulnerabilities, collectively named DifyTap, in Dify, the open-source agentic workflow platform with over 146,000 GitHub stars. Two were critical and two required no authentication, with cross-tenant flaws that let attackers read other customers' private AI chats and uploaded documents on the multi-tenant cloud service using only a file UUID.

title: How we found a bug in the hyper HTTP library
blurb: Cloudflare engineers spent six weeks tracing intermittent truncation in their Images binding, where larger transformation requests returned a 200 status but delivered only a fraction of the expected image data. The root cause was a race condition in the hyper Rust HTTP library affecting how processed image data streamed back to the client, and the fix came down to four lines of code.

title: Interactions API: our primary interface for Gemini models and agents
blurb: Google moved its Interactions API to general availability and made it the primary interface for calling Gemini models and running agents, replacing the December 2025 beta. The GA release adds Managed Agents that provision a remote Linux sandbox per call, background execution via background=True, mixed built-in and custom tools in one request, and Flex/Priority tiers that cut cost by up to 50%.

title: ShapedPlugin WordPress Pro Plugins Backdoored in Supply Chain Attack
blurb: Attackers compromised ShapedPlugin's build and distribution pipeline, injecting backdoor code into Pro versions of Product Slider Pro for WooCommerce, Real Testimonials Pro, and Smart Post Show Pro delivered through the vendor's official licensed update channels. The malware fetches a remote payload on every admin page load, captures plaintext credentials and 2FA codes, exfiltrates wp-config.php and recent WooCommerce order data, and carries a CVSS 10.0 rating, while free WordPress.org builds are unaffected.

title: Steam Machine launches today
blurb: Valve's Steam Machine ships today, a compact living-room PC running SteamOS on Linux with Proton handling Windows game compatibility. The launch extends Valve's push to make Linux a viable gaming target, building on the Steam Deck's Proton layer that has steadily closed the compatibility gap with native Windows titles.

title: The text in Claude Code’s “Extended Thinking” output
blurb: The author argues that Claude Code's local "Extended Thinking" logs are not an authentic record of the model's reasoning, since Anthropic encrypts the actual thinking into a signature it holds the key to and returns only a lossy summary over the API. The practical takeaway for engineers is to stop treating those local files as an audit trail, because reconstructing the real logic that drove an agent's actions requires an enterprise agreement and is impossible from your machine alone.

title: Show HN: Oak – Git alternative designed for agents
blurb: Oak is an open-source version control system built for AI coding agents, structured as a Rust Cargo workspace with a reusable VCS library and a CLI that agents drive. It uses branch-per-session as the unit of work, branch descriptions instead of per-commit messages, and content-addressed lazy mounts that hydrate on demand, making it faster than git for agent workloads.

title: Making secret scanning more trustworthy: Reducing false positives at scale
blurb: GitHub partnered with Microsoft Security & AI's Agents Offense team to add LLM-based contextual verification to its secret scanning, borrowing the in-context reasoning approach from Agentic Secret Finder to judge whether a detected value is a real exposure rather than just pattern-matching it. The goal is to bring AI-detected generic secrets up to the same precision as provider-pattern detections, cutting low-value alerts that erode developer trust without sacrificing coverage.

title: Adopting AV1 for Real-Time Communication (RTC) at Scale
blurb: Meta rolled out the AV1 video codec across the majority of mobile devices in its Messenger and WhatsApp real-time calling apps, after introducing it on high-end hardware in 2023. The post details the rate control and error resilience work behind the deployment, citing at least a 20% bitrate reduction over H.264/AVC on low- and mid-range devices and noticeably sharper video at the sub-100 kbps bitrates common in emerging markets.

title: Lighthouse agentic browsing scoring
blurb: Chrome's Lighthouse added an Agentic Browsing category that runs deterministic audits on how well a site supports machine interaction, checking WebMCP tool registration, accessibility-tree integrity, and layout stability. Rather than a 0-100 score, it reports a fractional pass ratio plus per-audit pass/fail status, and the CDP-driven checks are reproducible enough to wire into CI/CD.

title: Prompt Injection as Role Confusion
blurb: The author argues that prompt injection is fundamentally a role-confusion problem: because an LLM receives system prompts, user messages, tool outputs, and its own reasoning as one undifferentiated token stream, it cannot reliably tell its own thoughts from injected text. Framing roles as the core abstraction lets the post derive new attacks, explain existing mech-interp results, and predict when an injection will succeed.

title: Boost Inference Performance up to 15x on NVIDIA Blackwell Using DFlash Speculative Decoding
blurb: NVIDIA released DFlash, an open-source block-diffusion drafter for speculative decoding that boosts gpt-oss-120b inference up to 15x on Blackwell.

title: F3
blurb: F3 is a research-prototype columnar file format that aims to fix the layout limitations of Parquet and ORC while staying interoperable, using embedded Wasm decoders so readers can support custom encodings without a format rewrite. The project is a proof-of-concept tied to a 2025 paper, currently tested only on Debian and explicitly not meant for production use.

title: Unsloth GLM-5.2 – How to Run Locally
blurb: Unsloth released Dynamic GGUF quants of Z.ai's GLM-5.2, a 744B-parameter MoE with 40B active parameters and a 1M context window, letting the open model run on local hardware down to a 239GB 2-bit build that fits a 256GB unified-memory Mac. The 2-bit quant holds ~82% accuracy at 84% smaller than the full 1.5TB model, with 4-bit and 5-bit dynamic quants measuring near-lossless on KL divergence.

title: ClickHouse Ingestion at Scale: An Open-Source Zepto Engineering Story
blurb: Zepto rewrote core parts of the open-source ClickHouse Kafka Connect connector to add buffering and batching logic, boosting ingestion throughput by 45% while eliminating GC pauses and cutting ClickHouse insert pressure. The post traces how Confluent Cloud's managed black box blocked low-level broker and connector tuning at a sustained 10 MB/s, and details the two pull requests Zepto merged back upstream to fix the hidden performance killers.

title: GitHub Updates actions/checkout to Block Common Pwn Request Attack Patterns
blurb: GitHub's actions/checkout v7 now refuses to fetch fork pull request code in `pull_request_target` and `workflow_run` workflows by default, blocking the most common pwn request pattern that lets untrusted forks steal the privileged GITHUB_TOKEN and secrets. Effective June 18, 2026, with a backport to all supported major versions due July 16, workflow authors who need the old behavior must explicitly opt in via the `allow-unsafe-pr-checkout` flag.

title: Elevated error rate across multiple models
blurb: Anthropic's status page reported an elevated error rate affecting multiple Claude models, degrading API responses for applications built on those endpoints. Teams routing production traffic through Claude saw failed or retried requests during the window, a reminder that single-provider LLM integrations inherit the provider's uptime and benefit from fallback routing or graceful degradation.

title: Announcing DuckDB 1.5.4
blurb: DuckDB 1.5.4 (Variegata) is the fifth patch release in the 1.5 line, shipping bugfixes, security patches, and performance work alongside a parallel v1.4.5 LTS (Andium) release. Fixes include correctness bugs in VARIANT casts and MERGE INTO binding, several crash and memory-leak repairs in Arrow GeoArrow serialization and gzip writes, and allocator and Parquet stats-pruning improvements.

title: CCCL Runtime: A Modern C++ Runtime for CUDA
blurb: NVIDIA released CCCL runtime, a set of header-only C++ APIs (`<cuda/stream>`, `<cuda/buffer>`, `<cuda/launch>`) that reimplements core CUDA functionality like stream management, memory allocation, and kernel launches on top of the driver API. It targets the same role as the classic CUDA runtime but with modern C++ idioms for safer resource management and explicit dependencies, plus compatibility helpers for incremental adoption without rewriting surrounding CUDA runtime code.

title: Codex-maxxing for long-running work
blurb: In this OpenAI whitepaper, Jason Liu lays out strategies for running Codex as a persistent workspace that preserves context and sustains progress across multi-prompt projects. The guide covers breaking goals into verifiable steps, maintaining continuity across parallel workstreams, and deciding when to delegate execution to Codex versus when to keep a human in the loop.

title: Unlimited OCR: One-Shot Long-Horizon Parsing
blurb: Baidu released Unlimited-OCR, a document-parsing model that extends Deepseek-OCR to handle long, multi-page inputs in a single pass. It runs through HuggingFace transformers on CUDA GPUs, supports single-image, multi-page, and PDF parsing with a 32K-token output window, and ships with weights on both HuggingFace and ModelScope alongside an arXiv paper.

title: AWS Continuum offers devs help with securing code
blurb: AWS released Continuum, a service that continuously discovers, investigates, and remediates vulnerabilities across first-party and third-party code, generating remediation recommendations and proposing fixes that route through existing review workflows. It adds new capabilities like STRIDE-format threat modeling on top of penetration testing and code scanning borrowed from Security Agent, plus an opt-in "enforce mode" that autonomously fixes code lapses once it has learned a team's guardrails.

title: DifyTap Bugs Let Attackers 'Wiretap' AI Chat Histories
blurb: Researchers disclosed four Dify vulnerabilities, dubbed DifyTap, that let attackers wiretap AI chat histories and exfiltrate cross-tenant data; patched in 1.14.2.

title: Experimenting with the proposed Cross-Origin Storage API in Transformers.js
blurb: The post experiments with the proposed Cross-Origin Storage API to let browser-based ML apps share cached model files across origins, instead of re-downloading identical weights every time a new site loads the same Transformers.js model. The author shows that two apps on different origins each fetch the same 177 MB Whisper model separately, and demonstrates how the API would deduplicate that storage by content hash with explicit user permission.

title: Introducing OmniVec: An Open-Source Embedding Platform for AI Apps on Azure
blurb: Microsoft open-sourced OmniVec, a platform that keeps the vector embeddings of your operational data in sync as the source records change. You register a source, embedding model, and vector store, and OmniVec handles backfill, change tracking, batched model invocation, and retries, shipping with support for Azure Cosmos DB, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server, and deploying into your own Azure subscription on AKS.

title: The minimum viable unit of saleable software
blurb: The author argues that LLMs have shifted the buy-vs-build calculus but not eliminated the case for selling software, since LLM-built systems still demand dozens of feedback loops to reach quality and carry ongoing maintenance cost. His bet on turning the River side project into a business rests on cheap not meaning free, leaving room for polished, well-maintained packages that internal LLM clones rarely match.

title: Announcing the Public Preview of Integrated Embeddings in Azure Cosmos DB: Build AI Apps With Embeddings That Stay in Sync
blurb: Azure Cosmos DB's Integrated Embeddings, now in public preview, automatically generates and maintains vector embeddings as items are written and updated, so the stored vectors always reflect the current data. You configure it with an embeddingSource block in the container vector policy, specifying the source properties, a Microsoft Foundry embedding model, and the output path, which removes the separate change-tracking pipeline RAG apps normally build to keep embeddings in sync.

title: Don't rely on instructions, use Agent Hooks to enforce guardrails
blurb: The author argues that AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md instructions are unreliable guardrails because agents ignore them, and shows how Agent Hooks enforce rules deterministically instead. The post walks through two Claude Code hooks: a PreToolUse hook that blocks raw `<input>` tags by exiting with code 2 to feed stderr back as agent feedback, and a Stop hook that prevents the agent from claiming it's done while a design-system ratchet test fails.

title: Show HN: TikZ Editor – WYSIWYG editor for figures in LaTeX
blurb: TikZ Editor is a browser-based WYSIWYG tool for building TikZ figures, letting you draw diagrams visually while it generates the corresponding LaTeX code. It targets the usual friction of hand-writing TikZ coordinates and node positioning, with the canvas and emitted code staying in sync as you edit.

title: The Flat Curve Society
blurb: The author argues that as frontier models cross into dangerous capability, access to them will be restricted like nuclear weapons, with governments clamping down on the compute supply chain rather than the models themselves. Most companies and engineers will be left with capped, mediocre models or buy intelligence vending-machine style, sending specs to vendors who run the work on their own servers.

title: window.showDirectoryPicker opens up a whole new world
blurb: Chrome's window.showDirectoryPicker() lets a website request read/write access to a directory on the user's filesystem, enabling local-first apps that operate on the user's own files instead of cloud storage. The author used it with Claude to build browser-based photo management and a node-based compositing tool inspired by Apple's Shake, all reading and writing source files directly on disk.

title: Why Drawing Tablet Brands Won't Collaborate on Linux Floss Drivers
blurb: The author, who reviews drawing tablets on Linux, documents his failed push to get brands like Huion, XpPen, Gaomon, and Ugee to share hardware specs with the kernel's hid/input maintainers for open-source drivers. After reaching a technical contact who manages drivers across all four brands, he traces the wall to a business reality: the brands treat their proprietary driver structure as a competitive asset and see no upside in upstream FLOSS collaboration.

title: Metric Semantic Layer: How Lyft Governs and Scales Key Data Definitions
blurb: Lyft built an internal Metric Semantic Layer, a centralized Python package that stores every metric's plain-English description and definitive SQL in YAML configs and Jinja templates, ending the problem of teams using divergent definitions for the same metric. Updating a definition once propagates the change to every downstream application, with governance baked in through clear ownership, defined scope, and accountability that survives org changes and attrition.

title: Optimizing Models to Be Fast at Codegen
blurb: Morph trains workload-specific speculative drafters on coding output, lifting acceptance from 1.93x to 3.07x and serving open models like Qwen and DeepSeek faster.

title: Build real agentic apps using CUGA: two dozen working examples on a lightweight harness
blurb: CUGA, IBM's open-source agent harness, handles the planning, execution loop, tool calls, and state management so building an agent comes down to a tool list and a prompt, installed via pip install cuga. The companion cuga-apps repo ships two dozen single-file FastAPI apps wrapping one CugaAgent each, with configurable Fast, Balanced, and Accurate reasoning modes and a choice of local, Docker, or E2B sandboxes.

title: I am dreading our LLM-written incident report future
blurb: The author argues that using LLMs to write incident reports, not just gather their inputs, removes the thinking-while-writing step that forces engineers to confront whether their explanation actually fits the evidence. The result is a plausible, unchecked narrative that may invent system couplings or miss real interactions, which the author considers more dangerous than LLM coding because there is no test to catch the fabrication.

title: A 3D voxel game engine written in APL
blurb: A developer built an experimental 3D voxel game engine in APL, using the notation to drive a Vulkan, DirectX12, and Metal rendering backend.

title: Latitude launches open-source platform to monitor AI agents
blurb: Latitude released an open-source, MIT-licensed platform for monitoring AI agents in production, clustering thousands of live conversations into usage patterns, collapsing repeated failures into named signals, and exposing it all to coding agents through an MCP server. Teams can self-host it, promote saved semantic searches into monitors that flag patterns before they spread, and turn production conversations into datasets to verify a fix holds before shipping.

title: Announcing General availability of the Azure Cosmos DB vNext emulator
blurb: Microsoft made the Azure Cosmos DB vNext emulator generally available as a Docker image running on Linux, macOS, and Windows across x64 and ARM64, giving developers a local instance for inner-loop work and CI integration tests. GA adds vector search, OpenTelemetry support, and a bundled Cosmos DB Shell that auto-runs `.csh` scripts from `/init` at startup, moving dataset bootstrap into the container and dropping app-side readiness polling.

title: How to Rank Local LLMs by Cost per Correct Answer (Measured GPU Energy, 8 Ollama Models)
blurb: The author benchmarked 8 local Ollama models on one RTX 3090 by cost per correct answer, dividing metered GPU energy by the number of correct responses rather than counting tokens per euro. The mid-size gemma4:26b won at 96.9% accuracy for €0.013 per 1,000 correct answers, roughly 18x cheaper than qwen3:8b at fp16, while reasoning mode and full precision added energy cost without improving correctness on these deterministic tasks.

title: The Coming Loop
blurb: The author argues that agentic engineering is shifting from the in-agent tool loop to a harness-level loop, where a queue feeds tasks to a machine and an outer harness decides whether the work is actually finished or needs another session. He resists adopting it for code he cares about, citing models that write overly defensive, locally-reasoned code and his own unwillingness to ship systems he cannot fully explain.

title: AWS enters the context layer race with a graph that learns from agents, not manual curation
blurb: AWS announced a context intelligence stack for AI agents, led by AWS Context, a knowledge graph service that automatically maps relationships across existing data and refines itself based on which sources agents actually use. It publishes all metadata as Apache Iceberg to S3 Tables queryable via Athena, Redshift, or Spark, and ships alongside GA of S3 Annotations and a preview of skill assets in Glue Data Catalog.

title: Boost BigQuery with Python: Managed Python UDFs now generally available
blurb: Google made BigQuery's Managed Python UDFs generally available, letting teams run custom Python with libraries like NumPy, pandas, and scikit-learn directly inside SQL queries or BigFrames on serverless, auto-scaling compute. BigQuery handles container builds, security patching, and execution, so procedural logic, scientific computation, and external API enrichment that used to need separate infrastructure now run next to the data without managing images or compute services.

title: The analytics engineer in 2026: system designer, governance owner, AI context provider
blurb: dbt argues the analytics engineer's job has shifted from writing models to designing the system around them, as AI now handles scaffolding, first-draft docs, and boilerplate tests for the 72% of analytics engineers who code with AI assistance. The remaining work is the judgment AI can't do: defining metric grain, drawing domain boundaries, and structuring the semantic layer so downstream AI queries stay consistent. Governance outputs like contracts, tests, and column-level lineage become the role's measured deliverable.

title: The semantic debt crisis no one is talking about
blurb: The author argues that organizations carry "semantic debt" alongside technical debt: every metric built without a canonical definition, every filter hardcoded into a dashboard instead of the data layer, is a withdrawal that compounds until three teams present three defensible-but-conflicting numbers for the same metric. The fix is encoding business meaning into the data itself through a semantic layer, which the piece frames as the foundation that makes AI reliable in production rather than a modeling nicety.

title: The new database world according to Google: Inexact queries and AI in everything
blurb: Google's database leadership laid out a roadmap built on AI-embedded tooling and "inexact" queries, with a product exec predicting that humans won't directly use data platforms within three to five years. The bet is that agents, not analysts, become the primary query interface, pushing vendors toward natural-language and approximate-result systems rather than the exact, schema-bound SQL that data teams build on today.

title: Building Jaeger’s ClickHouse backend: 8.6× compression on 10 million spans
blurb: Jaeger v2.18.0 ships a native ClickHouse tracing backend, hitting 8.6× compression on 10M spans via columnar storage.

title: EDB converges analytics on Postgres to support AI agents
blurb: EDB introduced converged analytics for its managed EDB Postgres AI service, keeping Postgres as the operational source of truth and using Apache Iceberg as a shared catalog across ClickHouse, WarehousePG, and Spark so analytical engines query the same data without copies or ETL. The move follows Databricks' Neon-based LTAP from the opposite direction, betting that enterprises focused on data sovereignty want AI and analytics running on infrastructure they own and control.

title: What Fivetran + dbt Labs brought to Databricks Data + AI Summit (and what you can take home)
blurb: dbt Labs and Fivetran, now merged into one company, used Databricks Data + AI Summit to pitch Open Data Infrastructure for agents that query the warehouse continuously and need governed, tested, traceable context. The concrete takeaway is a customer pattern from Integral Ad Science that wires Databricks to dbt through MCP servers, letting an agent pull model metadata and lineage to answer why a BI number is wrong.

title: Introducing OmniVec: An Open-Source Embedding Platform for AI Apps on Azure
blurb: Microsoft open-sourced OmniVec, a platform that runs the embedding pipelines keeping vector representations of operational data in sync, with backfill, change tracking, model invocation, and retry logic handled across four primitives: source, model, destination, and pipeline. It deploys into your own Azure subscription with support for Cosmos DB, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server as sources, and replaces the bespoke change-capture plumbing most teams rebuild for every AI app.

title: A New Era of Midjourney
blurb: Midjourney announced a pivot beyond image generation into medical hardware, building a full-body Ultrasonic CT scanner that uses a ring of roughly 500,000 grain-sized sensors to produce MRI-quality 3D maps in a 60-second scan. The company plans to open a spa-styled scanning room in San Francisco in 2027 and scale to a fleet of 50,000 scanners handling a billion scans a month by 2031.

title: Why Drawing Tablet Brands Won't Collaborate on Linux Floss Drivers
blurb: Artist David Revoy explains why Huion, XPPen, Gaomon, and Ugee won't help build open-source Linux drivers: the four brands share a single technical team in Shenzhen, and contributing specs to the kernel's HID/input maintainers offers them no commercial upside while exposing how interchangeable their hardware is. The piece is a clear-eyed look at why community driver work still falls to volunteers dumping specs by hand, the way Wacom's decades of upstream support remains the lonely exception.

title: Here’s what’s new with iOS 27 beta 2
blurb: Apple released iOS 27 beta 2 to developers, the first follow-up since the WWDC debut, with refinements to Liquid Glass, a faster Siri AI, and a "Write with Siri" keyboard suggestion that replaces Writing Tools. The build feels further along than typical second betas, signaling that Apple is treating stability as a headline feature ahead of the July public beta and September release.

title: iOS 27 beta 2 includes Write with Siri, the new version of Apple Intelligence writing tools
blurb: Apple's iOS 27 beta 2 replaces Writing Tools with Write with Siri, swapping the old panel of preset buttons like 'Friendly' and 'Professional' for a natural-language input that expands from the Dynamic Island and pulls in personal context. The new system handles generation, proofreading, and rewrites with a before/after review panel, and works by voice or keyboard across iOS, iPadOS 27, and macOS Golden Gate ahead of a fall release.

title: Clap Studio creates "the most music-respectful room" in Spook nightclub
blurb: Clap Studio renovated the El Cubo room at Valencia's Spook nightclub by borrowing acoustic principles from "dead rooms," using sound-absorbing surfaces and minimal reflective materials to prioritize music clarity over visual spectacle. The project, a follow-up to the studio's work on sister club Oven, treats acoustics as the primary design constraint rather than an afterthought layered onto a finished interior.

title: ‘Hard Fork’ Live Part 2: Dylan Field on Standing Out in the A.I. Era
blurb: Figma CEO Dylan Field argued on a live "Hard Fork" episode that designers and writers with a distinct creative voice should take public risks now, framing the AI era as an opening rather than a threat. His pitch reframes the standout-or-fade anxiety around AI tooling as a case for putting personal work out there, betting that human voice becomes more valuable as generation gets cheaper.

title: Reader Submitted: An Assistive Communication Ecosystem Enabling Real-Time Deaf-Hearing Conversation
blurb: Dari is a student-designed assistive communication ecosystem built over a 26-week, co-design process with the Deaf community, combining an EMG armband (Woori) that reads sign-language gestures, smart glasses (Sari) that caption speech in the wearer's field of view, and a companion app. The concept's value is its insistence on two-way, real-time exchange that keeps both parties in their preferred mode rather than routing everything through an interpreter or one-way text.

title: FFmpeg fixes PixelSmash flaw in widely used video decoder
blurb: FFmpeg patched PixelSmash (CVE-2026-8461, CVSS 8.8), a heap out-of-bounds write in the MagicYUV decoder triggered by a malicious AVI, MKV, or MOV file processed through libavcodec. JFrog achieved full RCE against a Jellyfin 10.11.9 server via its automatic library scan; Kodi, OBS, PhotoPrism, Nextcloud, and desktop thumbnail generators are also exposed, with RCE viable when ASLR is disabled or chained around.

title: Nearly half of LG smart TV apps contain residential proxy SDKs
blurb: Researchers at Spur scanned 6,038 LG and Samsung smart TV apps and found 2,058 of them, nearly half on LG, bundling residential proxy SDKs from vendors like Bright Data that route strangers' internet traffic through the TV's home connection. The SDKs ask for consent once during setup and keep proxying after the app is closed, often with the proxy company itself acting as publisher, making these devices long-lived, unaudited exit nodes on home networks.

title: A new unpatchable flaw in Apple chips opens the door to an iPhone jailbreak
blurb: Paradigm Shift, a Barcelona offensive-security firm, published "usbliter8," a Boot ROM flaw with a proof-of-concept affecting Apple's A12 and A13 chips in the iPhone XS, XR, and 11. Because the bug sits in immutable, burned-in code it cannot be patched, but exploitation requires physical access and must be chained with further bugs to achieve a full jailbreak or forensic unlock.

title: LastPass confirms data breach in Klue supply chain attack
blurb: LastPass confirmed attackers accessed customer data in its Salesforce environment after the Icarus extortion group stole OAuth tokens from Klue, a third-party market intelligence platform integrated with LastPass's go-to-market systems. Exposed data includes names, phone numbers, email and physical addresses, and CRM records, but products, infrastructure, and customer vaults were unaffected; LastPass has rotated the exposed tokens and warns of phishing from attacker-controlled domains.

title: Researchers Detail DifyTap Flaws in Dify That Could Expose AI Chats Across Tenants
blurb: Zafran Security disclosed four vulnerabilities, collectively named DifyTap, in Dify, the open-source agentic workflow platform, two of them critical (CVE-2026-41948 at CVSS 9.4, CVE-2026-41947 at 9.1) and two requiring no authentication, that allow cross-tenant reads of other customers' AI chats and uploaded files on Dify's multi-tenant cloud. Operators on affected versions should patch immediately and treat prior cross-tenant conversation and document exposure as possible, since the flaws created a covert exfiltration channel.

title: ShapedPlugin WordPress Pro Plugins Backdoored in Supply Chain Attack
blurb: Threat actors compromised ShapedPlugin's build and EDD distribution pipeline, backdooring Pro releases of Product Slider Pro for WooCommerce (CVE-2026-49777, CVSS 10.0), Real Testimonials Pro, and Smart Post Show Pro (incident-wide CVE-2026-10735, CVSS 9.8). The loader fetches a self-erasing payload that captures plaintext credentials and 2FA codes, exfiltrates wp-config.php and admin accounts, and drops a web shell; only Pro builds from account.shapedplugin.com are affected.

title: Eight-Year-Old Samsung KNOX Flaw Exposed Millions of Galaxy Devices to Kernel Attacks
blurb: Samsung patched CVE-2026-20971 (CVSS 7.8), an eight-year-old use-after-free in KNOX affecting nearly every Galaxy device from the S9 to S25, including A-series and both Exynos and Qualcomm variants. LucidBit Labs triggered the race condition from an untrusted app by loading a non-ELF file to bypass KCFI protections, achieving controlled kernel memory corruption; the fix shipped in Samsung's January 2026 update.

title: Microsoft fixes AutoGen Studio flaw that enabled code execution
blurb: Microsoft patched AutoJack, a three-flaw chain in AutoGen Studio that let a malicious webpage drive a developer's browsing agent into running arbitrary PowerShell, Bash, or executables on the host via the unauthenticated localhost MCP WebSocket. Exposure was limited to those building from the main GitHub branch between the MCP plugin commit and the hardening fix; PyPI releases, including autogenstudio 0.4.2.2, were never affected.

title: Jaredfromsubway.eth, Ethereum's Most Active Sandwich Bot, Drained for $7.5M Over the Weekend
blurb: An attacker drained over $7.5 million in WETH, USDC, and USDT from jaredfromsubway.eth, the Ethereum address responsible for roughly 70% of all sandwich attacks on the network, using what Blockaid called a counter-MEV honeypot rather than phishing or a private-key compromise. It is the largest single-event loss publicly tied to an MEV operator, and some proceeds have already moved through Tornado Cash.

title: FFmpeg PixelSmash Flaw Allows RCE on Video Players, Media Servers, NAS Appliances
blurb: FFmpeg's libavcodec contains a heap out-of-bounds write in the MagicYUV decoder (CVE-2026-8461, CVSS 8.8), dubbed PixelSmash by JFrog, exploitable for unauthenticated RCE via a crafted 50KB AVI, MKV, or MOV file with no user privileges required. Because FFmpeg backs most media pipelines, the bug triggers when video players, file-manager thumbnailers, upload-processing servers, NAS appliances, or smart TVs decode the file, enabling zero-click attacks through torrent clients that auto-scan media folders.

title: Daybreak: Tools for securing every organization in the world
blurb: OpenAI expanded its Daybreak program with new defensive tooling, including an updated Codex Security plugin that discovers and patches vulnerabilities, and the full release of GPT-5.5-Cyber, which scores 85.6% on CyberGym versus 81.8% for GPT-5.5, available through a limited release to trusted defenders. It also launched Patch the Planet with Trail of Bits and HackerOne, with cURL, Go, Python, and 30-plus open-source projects committed to AI-assisted patching.

title: FortiBleed Attackers Turn Firewalls Into Credential Stealers as Heists Persist
